chunk_summary: Age-banded counseling to reduce ultra-processed food (UPF) exposure focuses on tailored dietary advice for children and adolescents, emphasizing practical swaps, label literacy, and linking dietary changes to valued outcomes like growth, stamina, and concentration.
learning_objectives: Understand the age-specific strategies for reducing ultra-processed food consumption in children and adolescents.
Learn practical dietary swaps and counseling techniques to promote minimally processed foods.
Recognize the importance of follow-up and family feasibility in dietary interventions.
Identify links between UPF reduction and health outcomes such as growth, sleep, and sports performance.
key_facts: For ages 1–5 years, replace one packaged snack daily with minimally processed options like fruit, roasted chana, or plain curd with fruit.
For ages 6–10 years, focus on school tiffin templates (staple + protein + vegetable/fruit) and teach label literacy for shorter ingredient lists and lower sugar/salt content.
For ages 11–18 years, link UPF reduction to outcomes like sports stamina, acne, and concentration, and aim for goals like 'two home meals daily' and 'no sugary drinks on weekdays.'
Follow-up every 4–8 weeks to reassess UPF frequency, growth, and family feasibility.
In resource-limited settings, prioritize affordable staples like dal and seasonal produce over premium 'health' packaged foods.
reference_quote: Ultra-processed diets cause excess calorie intake and weight gain — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7959109/
